meta.source: Curated seed dataset for UI demonstration; replace with NASA Exoplanet Archive TAP output by running scripts/fetch_exoplanets.py
meta.notes: Seed values are rounded and intentionally compact so the site works immediately. Use the included updater for an official archive-derived JSON catalog.

planets.name: 51 Pegasi b
planets.description: The first confirmed planet discovered around a Sun-like star, famous for showing that giant planets can orbit extremely close to their hosts.

planets.name: HD 209458 b
planets.description: A benchmark transiting hot Jupiter; its transit made radius, density, and atmospheric follow-up studies possible.

planets.name: GJ 876 b
planets.description: A giant planet around a nearby red dwarf, important for early multi-planet radial-velocity dynamics.

planets.name: 55 Cancri e
planets.description: A very hot super-Earth orbiting its star in less than one Earth day.

planets.name: 2M1207 b
planets.description: One of the earliest directly imaged planetary-mass companions, orbiting a young brown dwarf.

planets.name: CoRoT-7 b
planets.description: A short-period rocky-size planet that helped establish small transiting exoplanets as a major research target.

planets.name: GJ 1214 b
planets.description: A nearby sub-Neptune whose flat transmission spectrum made it a major atmospheric-composition case study.

planets.name: WASP-12 b
planets.description: An intensely irradiated hot Jupiter often used to study atmospheric escape and tidal effects.

planets.name: HR 8799 b
planets.description: Part of a directly imaged multi-planet system that became a reference point for young giant exoplanets.

planets.name: HR 8799 c
planets.description: A directly imaged giant planet in the HR 8799 system, useful for atmospheric and orbital architecture studies.

planets.name: HR 8799 d
planets.description: One of several directly imaged young giant planets orbiting HR 8799.

planets.name: HR 8799 e
planets.description: The inner directly imaged planet in the HR 8799 system, discovered after the first three planets.

planets.name: Beta Pictoris b
planets.description: A young directly imaged giant planet orbiting within the debris-disk environment of Beta Pictoris.

planets.name: Kepler-10 b
planets.description: One of Kepler’s first confirmed rocky-size planets, orbiting extremely close to its star.

planets.name: Kepler-22 b
planets.description: A Kepler planet with an Earth-like orbital period around a Sun-like star, though larger than Earth.

planets.name: Kepler-186 f
planets.description: A small planet in a compact Kepler system, often highlighted as an early Earth-size habitable-zone candidate.

planets.name: Kepler-452 b
planets.description: A super-Earth-size Kepler planet with an orbit comparable in duration to Earth’s year.

planets.name: K2-18 b
planets.description: A nearby sub-Neptune around an M dwarf, widely studied because its atmosphere can be observed in transit.

planets.name: Proxima Centauri b
planets.description: The nearest known exoplanet candidate/confirmed planet around the closest star to the Sun, detected by radial velocity.

planets.name: TRAPPIST-1 b
planets.description: The innermost planet in the seven-planet TRAPPIST-1 system.

planets.name: TRAPPIST-1 c
planets.description: A close-orbiting terrestrial-size planet in the resonant TRAPPIST-1 architecture.

planets.name: TRAPPIST-1 d
planets.description: A small planet in the TRAPPIST-1 system, valuable for studying densities in compact systems.

planets.name: TRAPPIST-1 e
planets.description: A terrestrial-size TRAPPIST-1 planet frequently discussed in comparative habitability studies.

planets.name: TRAPPIST-1 f
planets.description: A terrestrial-size planet in the outer part of the resonant TRAPPIST-1 chain.

planets.name: TRAPPIST-1 g
planets.description: A terrestrial-size planet in the TRAPPIST-1 system with a longer period than the inner planets.

planets.name: TRAPPIST-1 h
planets.description: The outermost known planet of the TRAPPIST-1 system.

planets.name: LHS 1140 b
planets.description: A nearby transiting super-Earth around a cool dwarf, notable for follow-up atmospheric interest.

planets.name: HIP 65426 b
planets.description: A young directly imaged giant planet later used as a high-contrast imaging target for space telescopes.

planets.name: TOI-700 d
planets.description: An Earth-size TESS planet in a system that became a prominent nearby small-planet target.

planets.name: AU Mic b
planets.description: A young planet orbiting an active nearby red dwarf with a debris disk.

planets.name: GJ 486 b
planets.description: A nearby transiting rocky planet used for studies of whether hot terrestrial planets can retain atmospheres.

planets.name: TOI-1452 b
planets.description: A TESS-discovered super-Earth-size planet around a nearby M dwarf system.

planets.name: TOI-715 b
planets.description: A nearby transiting super-Earth-size TESS planet around a cool star.

planets.name: LTT 1445 A b
planets.description: A nearby transiting planet in a multiple-star system, useful for follow-up observations.

planets.name: WASP-39 b
planets.description: A Saturn-mass transiting planet that became a showcase target for atmospheric spectroscopy.

planets.name: GJ 3470 b
planets.description: A warm Neptune-size planet around an M dwarf, often discussed in atmospheric escape studies.

planets.name: OGLE-2005-BLG-390L b
planets.description: A cold, distant planet discovered through gravitational microlensing rather than host-star light curves or spectra.

planets.name: Kepler-16 b
planets.description: A Saturn-size planet orbiting two stars, demonstrating that stable circumbinary planets are observable by transit timing and geometry.
